infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(East_Asian)#Archaic_Hangul "Help:Multilingual support (East Asian)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Tai Tham script](/wiki/Tai_Tham_script "Tai Tham script").

([Northern Thai](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language"): ᨾᩯ᩵ᩁᩬ᩵ᨦᩈᩬᩁ; [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): แม่ฮ่องสอน, pronounced [[mɛ̂ː hɔ̂ŋ sɔ̌ːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is a town (*[thesaban mueang](/wiki/Thesaban_mueang "Thesaban mueang")*) in north-west [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), capital of [Mae Hong Son Province](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Province "Mae Hong Son Province"). It is in the [Shan Hills](/wiki/Shan_Hills "Shan Hills"), near the border with [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") along the banks of the River [Pai](/wiki/Pai_(river) "Pai (river)"). As of 2018, the town had 7,066 inhabitants.[[1]](#cite_note-TDD-1) The town covers the *[tambon](/wiki/Tambon "Tambon")* Chong Kham of the [Mueang Mae Hong Son district](/wiki/Amphoe_Mueang_Mae_Hong_Son "Amphoe Mueang Mae Hong Son"). Mae Hong Son is 883 kilometres (549 mi) north of Bangkok and 251 kilometres (156 mi) northwest of Chiang Mai.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Town in Thailand

This article is about the town Mae Hong Son. For other uses, see [Mae Hong Son (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_(disambiguation) "Mae Hong Son (disambiguation)").

Town in Mae Hong Son Province, Thailand

**This article contains [Lanna text](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(East_Asian)#Archaic_Hangul "Help:Multilingual support (East Asian)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Tai Tham script](/wiki/Tai_Tham_script "Tai Tham script").

**Mae Hong Son** ([Northern Thai](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language"): ᨾᩯ᩵ᩁᩬ᩵ᨦᩈᩬᩁ; [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): แม่ฮ่องสอน, pronounced [[mɛ̂ː hɔ̂ŋ sɔ̌ːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is a town (*[thesaban mueang](/wiki/Thesaban_mueang "Thesaban mueang")*) in north-west [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), capital of [Mae Hong Son Province](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Province "Mae Hong Son Province"). It is in the [Shan Hills](/wiki/Shan_Hills "Shan Hills"), near the border with [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") along the banks of the River [Pai](/wiki/Pai_(river) "Pai (river)"). As of 2018, the town had 7,066 inhabitants.[[1]](#cite_note-TDD-1) The town covers the *[tambon](/wiki/Tambon "Tambon")* Chong Kham of the [Mueang Mae Hong Son district](/wiki/Amphoe_Mueang_Mae_Hong_Son "Amphoe Mueang Mae Hong Son"). Mae Hong Son is 883 kilometres (549 mi) north of Bangkok and 251 kilometres (156 mi) northwest of Chiang Mai.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mae_Hong_Son&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The territory of Mae Hong Son Province was formerly part of [Mawkmai State](/wiki/Mawkmai_State "Mawkmai State"), one of the [Shan States](/wiki/Shan_States "Shan States") which had been founded in 1767 by Hsai Khiao, hailing from a noble family of [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

As a result of the [Anglo-Siamese Boundary Commission](/w/index.php?title=Anglo-Siamese_Boundary_Commission&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anglo-Siamese Boundary Commission (page does not exist)") of 1892-93 Mae Hong Son district was ceded to [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) but the adjacent Möngmaü and Mehsakun trans-[Salween](/wiki/Salween "Salween") districts— also claimed by Siam as territories located on the eastern side of the Salween River— were kept as part of [British Burma](/wiki/British_Burma "British Burma").[[4]](#cite_note-IGI-4)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mae_Hong_Son&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Climate")]

Mae Hong Son has a [tropical savanna climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Aw*). Winters are dry and warm. Temperatures rise until April, which is very hot with the average daily maximum at 38.7 °C (101.7 °F). The [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") season runs from May through October, with heavy rain and somewhat cooler temperatures during the day, although nights remain warm. Mae Hong Son holds the highest temperature ever recorded in Thailand when 44.6 °C (112.3 °F) was observed on 28 April 2016.[[5]](#cite_note-meteofranceasia-5)

| Source 2: Thai Meteorological Department (Feb–May record highs 1951-2023 and Nov-Feb record lows 1951–2022, extremes 1981-2010)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-extreme_minimum-10)[[11]](#cite_note-TMD1-11) Office of Water Management and Hydrology, Royal Irrigation Department (sun 1981–2010)[[12]](#cite_note-RID-12) | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mae_Hong_Son&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Mae Hong Son can be reached by car or bus from [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai") by the [Mae Hong Son loop](https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Loop "voy:Mae Hong Son Loop"). The town is also serviced by the [Mae Hong Son Airport](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Airport "Mae Hong Son Airport"). It is also home to the only commercial Diesel power station in Thailand. The station has only a very small capacity of 4.40 MW (4,400 kW). It seems like it was placed here because of the remote location of the town.

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mae_Hong_Son&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tourism")]

The town has some tourist infrastructure, including many guesthouses and several internet cafes. It also has a [vegetarian](/wiki/Vegetarian "Vegetarian") restaurant near the main market.

It is a popular jumping-off point for tours to visit [hill tribe](/wiki/Hill_tribe_(Thailand) "Hill tribe (Thailand)") villages, caves, and waterfalls in the area. There are also some hot springs nearby.

"Thung Dok Bua Tong" in Amphoe Khun Yuam, where the fields of wild sunflowers bloom and cover the hills during the month of November, is best reached from Mae Hong Son and lies some 80 km away.
